title: What Is npm?
subtitle: The world's largest code library

sections.content:
When you installed Node.js, you got a bonus tool called **npm** — the Node Package Manager.

npm is like an app store, but for code. Instead of downloading apps to your phone, you download **packages** (reusable chunks of code) to your project. Need to build a web server? There's a package for that. Need to format dates? There's a package for that. Need to send emails? Package.

Instead of writing everything from scratch, developers stand on the shoulders of millions of other developers who've shared their work for free.
sections.analogy: npm is like a massive LEGO store. Instead of carving every brick yourself, you grab pre-made pieces and snap them together to build something new.

sections.content: The npm registry has over **2 million packages** — and it grows every day. Here are some of the most popular ones you'll encounter:
keyPoints: express — build web servers (downloaded 30+ million times per week)
axios — make HTTP requests to other servers and APIs
dayjs — work with dates and times without going insane
chalk — add colors to your terminal output
sections.tip: You can browse all packages at npmjs.com. Every package has a page with documentation, download stats, and a link to its source code.

sections.question: What is npm?
options: A text editor for writing code
A package manager that lets you install reusable code libraries
A website for hosting your projects
sections.explanation: npm (Node Package Manager) is a tool for installing, managing, and sharing reusable JavaScript packages. It came bundled with Node.js when you installed it. It's not a language, editor, or hosting service — it's a package manager.

sections.content:
You'll use npm constantly as a developer. The two commands you'll use most:

- `npm install <package>` — download a package into your project
- `npm init` — set up a new project

Let's put these to work in the next lesson.
keyPoints: npm install downloads packages from the registry
npm init creates a new project configuration
Every Node.js project has a package.json file that lists its packages

completionMessage: Now you know what npm is — a massive library of free, reusable code. Next, you'll create your first real project and start using it.
